Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A

journal-title-group.journal-title: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title-group.article-title: Climate warming increases global oceanic dimethyl sulfide emissions

contrib-group.aff: aIndian Institute of Tropical Meteorology, Ministry of Earth Sciences, Pune 411008, India
bDepartment of Atmospheric and Space Sciences, Savitribai Phule Pune University, Pune 411007, India

dOceanography Department, Faculty of Science, Alexandria University, Alexandria 21500, Egypt
eInstitut de Ciències del Mar, Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Científicas, Barcelona, Catalonia 08003, Spain

author-notes.corresp: 1To whom correspondence may be addressed. Email: anoop@tropmet.res.in.

license.license-p: This article is distributed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives License 4.0 (CC BY-NC-ND).

abstract.title: Significance
abstract.p: This work reduces the current large differences and biases observed in the previous outcomes of the CMIP6 models in terms of the future changes in the seawater dimethyl sulfide (DMS) concentrations and sea-air flux estimates. This not only increases our confidence in predicting future changes in DMS emissions but also addresses the current uncertainty in climate-DMS feedback impacts in the IPCC AR6. The results show that seawater DMS will decrease in the future, but the sea-air emissions will increase. This suggests that DMS will play an increasingly important role as a sulfur source as further air quality measures reduce anthropogenic emissions of sulfur compounds.

Oceanic dimethyl sulfide (DMS) is the largest natural source of atmospheric sulfur. DMS is biologically produced in seawater and emitted into the atmosphere, where its oxidation products contribute to aerosol formation with consequences for cloud albedo and the Earth’s radiative budget and climate. Climate model projections of how DMS emissions change with global warming are largely uncertain, even contradictory. Here, we use machine-learning models trained with biome-resolved global observations to simulate seawater DMS concentrations (1850 to 2100) using physico-chemical and biological predictors from eight CMIP6 models. The scatter in current projections is largely reduced, and globally averaged seawater DMS concentrations are predicted to decrease in the coming decades. However, global DMS emissions will increase due to rising surface wind speeds and sea surface temperatures which contradicts the current AR6 assessment that the DMS flux will reduce in the future. Concurrence of increasing DMS emissions and declining anthropogenic sulfur dioxide emissions suggests an increase in the relative importance of DMS to sulfate aerosol formation and its climate cooling impact.

body.p: DMS is formed in seawater from its precursor dimethylsulfoniopropionate (DMSP), produced mostly by phytoplankton (1). Biotic and abiotic factors responsible for the taxonomy, lifecycle, and physiology of phytoplankton exert control over the production of DMSP (2, 3). Once released into seawater, DMSP is degraded via three pathways (4): a) generation of DMS via enzymatic cleavage, b) assimilation of DMSP-sulfur via the demethylation pathway, releasing methanethiol (MeSH), and c) the generation of dimethylsulfoxonium propionate. The resultant DMS and MeSH are then emitted into the atmosphere. On average, ca. 80% of the ocean-atmosphere flux of volatile sulfur is in the form of DMS (5).

In the atmosphere, DMS undergoes photochemical transformation, eventually contributing to sulfate aerosol formation, which can affect cloud albedo, the Earth’s radiation budget and climate (6). The globally and annually averaged effect of DMS on the aerosol radiative effect has been estimated at ca. −1.8 W m−2, which is comparable in magnitude to the anthropogenic CO2 warming effect (1.83 ± 0.2 W m−2) (4), while regionally, it can exceed −20 W m−2 (7). Whether this climate cooling effect of DMS will increase or decrease with global warming in the coming decades is largely unknown. This is because the magnitude, and even the sign, of projected changes in DMS emissions, are not well constrained (8). Hitherto, most models compute current oceanic DMS flux using climatological seawater DMS estimates (9–11) and a sea-air flux parameterization (12, 13). The challenge for DMS flux projections is to accurately represent the evolution of the global distribution of the surface-ocean DMS concentrations (14, 15) with global warming.

The DMS flux can change due to alterations in sea surface temperature (SST), solar radiation (Rad), ocean mixed-layer depth (MLD), sea ice extent and thickness, windspeed, nutrient distribution, or pH (16). In the IPCC AR5 report (17), the change in the DMS radiative impact due to global warming was estimated to be −0.02 W m−2 °C−1, suggesting small negative feedback (18). Since AR5, modeling studies using experimentally based relationships between oceanic variables and DMS production have suggested a positive climate feedback, meaning decreased fluxes in response to warming (19–21). However, this is far from certain considering the complex and compensating regional alterations in marine ecosystems that can hardly be tackled with experimental approaches (22). The latest AR6 estimated the climate change-related DMS effect to be +0.005 (0.0 to 0.01) W m−2 °C−1 (23). This estimate was guided by models that try to reproduce either the biogeochemical DMS formation and loss processes (prognostic approach) or the statistically relevant DMS drivers (diagnostic approach) (24, 25). Due to incomplete knowledge, these models provide diverging estimates of changes in DMS. Out of four Earth System models of the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) that included DMS, two models (one prognostic, one diagnostic) predicted an increase, while the other two models predicted a decrease in DMS emission during the 21st century.

Recently, machine learning-based approaches have shown an improvement in estimating seawater DMS using satellite-based proxy variables (26–31). However, these approaches do not provide information on early historical (pre-1990) and future changes as they depend on available satellite data. Here, we estimate seawater DMS concentrations and fluxes in the past, present, and future (1850 to 2100) for four shared socioeconomic pathways (32) (SSP126: temperature rise by 1.8 °C by 2100; SSP245: 2.7 °C; SSP370: 3.6 °C; and SSP585: 4.4 °C; see Materials and Methods) using machine learning-based models trained with observed DMS, physical and biogeochemical predictors from eight CMIP6 models. Our results greatly reduce the scatter in the current projections and show that globally averaged seawater DMS concentrations are predicted to decrease in the coming decades, but the sea-air fluxes are predicted to increase.

sec.title: Performance of the Machine Learning Models.
sec.p: Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) models were applied to eight CMIP6 models (SI Appendix, Table S1) to predict seawater DMS concentrations (see Materials and Methods and Fig. 1). Seawater DMS was estimated in each biome using predictor variables from the CMIP6 models. We only included predictor variables that performed with 95% significance with respect to in situ DMS observations in each biome for each model (SI Appendix, Table S2). The performances of each GPR model in each biome are shown in SI Appendix, Figs. S1–S4. A comparison between in situ observations from 1972 to 2014 with the predicted multimodel GPR ensemble monthly DMS shows that in all the biomes, except the Coastal biome, the Pearson correlation coefficient (R) ranges from 0.80 to 0.86 and globally the R-value is 0.86 (SI Appendix, Fig. S5). In contrast, the same comparison with the ensemble predictions of the current DMS biogeochemical model estimations gave R-values in the range of 0.17 to 0.33 in the individual biomes and 0.31 globally (SI Appendix, Fig. S6). Hence, the GPR models led to a major improvement in reducing the DMS bias when compared to observations. In the Coastal biome, the distribution is skewed slightly to higher concentrations, but in the other biomes and at a global scale, the distribution in the ensemble predicted DMS is similar to the in-situ observations (SI Appendix, Fig. S5). The GPR model performs better than CMIP6 model results also in terms of RMSE (SI Appendix, Figs. S5 and S6). The GPR models still fail to predict very high DMS concentrations (>150 nM), suggesting that predictions are a lower limit in certain regions. This is not surprising as such concentrations and associated high fluxes tend to be sporadic, small-scale, and short-term (33), which cannot be represented adequately with monthly means and coarse-resolution global models. For example, the Trade and Westerlies biomes (SI Appendix, Figs. S3 and S4) show lower RMSE errors than the Coastal biome. This is due to higher seawater DMS variability and poor spatial resolution of the predictors in the Coastal biome. The mean bias in the Coastal biome is still less than 0.1 nM, and the R-value is higher than 0.7, signaling that the GPR models can capture most seawater DMS variations across different environments much better than the current biogeochemistry models. Furthermore, the GPR approach shows a clear improvement in the Southern Ocean region (SI Appendix, Fig. S7) compared to a satellite-based diagnostic DMS model, which showed a large bias relative to observations (34). The Polar biome, which is one of the DMS hotspot regions, shows the best-fitted predictions by the GPR model.

caption.p: Global seawater DMS concentrations were predicted using GPR models trained with biome-resolved in situ observations and biogeochemical and physical variables from eight CMIP6 models. (A) Global distribution of in situ DMS observations (plotted as colored dots) used for training the GPR models. The data are separated according to oceanic biomes (Coastal, Polar, Trade, and Westerlies; plotted in the background) before training the GPR models. (B) The GPR models are trained for each biome separately and the seawater DMS is predicted for individual biomes using the CMIP6 biogeochemical variables for each month from 1850 to 2100. (C) Finally, the global seawater DMS distribution is obtained by combining the predicted results from each biome for each month as shown in (B). This figure shows an example of the predicted biome-wise and global seawater DMS distribution in December 2000 for the MPI-ESM1-2-HR model.

sec.title: Drivers of Seawater DMS Concentrations.
sec.p: The time series of the predictor variables of each model (SI Appendix, Figs. S8–S15) show that the global annual average values decrease over the 21st century, except for SST and solar radiation. However, the absolute trends are different for each variable. This suggests that the estimated change in DMS concentration is not driven by a single variable but rather by concerted changes in multiple variables. This is in line with the knowledge that the production and consumption of DMS are determined by a variety of processes involving both biotic and abiotic factors (35, 36). The relative contribution of each predictor variable varies from biome to biome, which is evident in the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis results (SI Appendix, Figs. S16–S20). The varying impact of predictors depicted by SHAP helps to explain the heterogeneity in DMS trends (SI Appendix, Fig. S21A). This agrees with the observation that the dependence of DMS on ocean variables changes geographically (37) and is the reason why we trained our GPR models by biomes rather than at a global scale. In the Coastal and Polar biomes, solar radiation is the most important driver for DMS (SI Appendix, Figs. S16–S18). Past studies have shown that solar radiation stimulates DMS production through stress responses (38–40). In the Trade biome, most regions are oligotrophic, and hence, the production of DMS is limited by nutrients (SI Appendix, Figs. S16 and S19). In the Westerlies biome, solar radiation again plays an important role along with MLD and SST. Interestingly, even though some of the predictors show large interannual variability even in the historical scenario (1850 to 2014), the magnitude of variability in seawater DMS is comparatively low (SI Appendix, Fig. S21A vs. SI Appendix, Figs. S8–S15). This shows that the contribution of multiple drivers, with different magnitudes and signs, can buffer the effect of individual drivers on seawater DMS concentrations. Indeed, a recent study has shown that the last interglacial period shows a small decrease in biogenic sulfur compared to the penultimate glacial period in the Southern Ocean(41), which also suggests that buffering mechanisms play a role in regulating DMS fluxes.

sec.title: Distribution and Trends in Seawater DMS Concentrations.
sec.p: The historical values of the annual global mean of seawater DMS concentrations vary between 1.67 nM and 1.82 nM across models (Fig. 2A). All models show a consistent trend both in the historical and future scenarios, although some differences are observed in the absolute values. The ensemble of all GPR implementations shows a global mean concentration of 1.77 ± 0.03 nM for the recent historical period since the beginning of in situ observations (1972 to 2014). This is lower than the global mean of 2.28 nM predicted by the latest data-interpolation-based seawater DMS climatology (9) but is within the range (1.51 to 1.98 nM) of previous estimates (8, 14, 34). The seasonal/geographical distribution is similar to previous estimates, where higher DMS is observed in the Arctic and Southern Oceans during their respective summers (SI Appendix, Fig. S22).

caption.p: Multimodel ensemble results show that global seawater DMS concentrations will decrease in the future, but the sea-air fluxes will show an increase. (A) The GPR multimodel ensemble of globally averaged annual seawater DMS concentrations predicted from the eight CMIP6 models and the associated standard deviation (SD) are shown for the historical past and the four future scenarios (SSP126, SSP245, SSP370, and SSP585). Global average annual seawater DMS concentrations show a decrease in all the scenarios, with concentrations stabilizing in the SSP126 and SSP245 scenarios after 2050, while they continue to decrease in the SSP370 and SSP585 scenarios. (B) The multimodel ensemble globally integrated ocean-atmosphere DMS flux along with the SD for the historical and future scenarios shows an increase even though the average seawater concentrations reduces due to the increase in seawater DMS concentrations over high-flux regions and an increase in the windspeed and SST in the future.

sec.p: The global average seawater DMS concentrations change over time driven by changes in oceanic variables (Fig. 2A). The rate of change in DMS during 1850 to 1900 was +0.07 ± 0.02% decade−1 but showed no significant change during 1900 to 1950. However, in the recent past, the global annual seawater DMS concentrations have been increasing (SI Appendix, Fig. S23–S26) at a rate of +0.06 ± 0.02% decade–1 (1950 to 2014). DMS concentration increases over the period 1998-2010 have been reported from bias-prone, satellite-based proxies (14), although at faster rates compared to our estimates (+2.1 to +3.8% decade–1).

For the future scenarios, (Summary given in SI Appendix, Table S5) all the models show a decrease in the global annual mean seawater DMS concentrations in the near future (2015 to 2050) (SSP126: –0.41 ± 0.04% decade–1, SSP245: –0.44 ± 0.04% decade–1, SSP370: –0.39 ± 0.06% decade–1, SSP585: –0.46 ± 0.03% decade–1) after which, depending on scenario, concentrations either continue to decrease (SSP245: –0.24 ± 0.03% decade−1, SSP370: −0.46 ± 0.03% decade−1 and SSP585: −0.58 ± 0.02% decade−1) or stagnate (SSP126: 0.02 ± 0.02% decade−1) from 2050 to 2100 (SI Appendix, Fig. S23–S26). Zhao et al. (21) also projected a decrease of −15.1% (with a range from −7.0 to −26.3%) in seawater DMS by 2100 for the SSP585 scenario. There is some variability among the individual GPR results (SI Appendix, Fig. S21), with the largest decline shown by CESM2-WACCM for the SSP585 scenario (−8%) and the lowest decrease shown by CMCC-ESM2 and CNRM-ESM2-1 (−0.6%, also for SSP585) for 2085 to 2100 compared to 1850 to 1865. The multimodel ensemble mean shows a relative decrease by 2100 ranging from −1.7% (SSP126) to −4.0% (SSP585).

While a decrease is projected in the annual global mean DMS concentrations for most future scenarios, a space-resolved look over 1850 to 2014 for historical and 2015 to 2100 for future scenarios shows that this decrease is dominated by a few regions (Fig. 3A; GPR results for the individual CMIP6 models from which predictor variables are used, are shown in SI Appendix, Figs. S27–S31). The northern polar region shows an increasing trend while decreasing trends dominate the Southern Ocean. The rise of seawater DMS in the Arctic is expected as sea ice melts in a warmer Earth, leading to changes in ocean (42) and sea ice (43) productivity and a large increase in the open water regions capable of producing and emitting DMS (44). Other regions, such as the northern tropical Atlantic and Pacific regions, also show an increase in DMS. However, globally this increase is counterbalanced by large areas showing a decrease in seawater DMS concentrations, particularly in DMS hotspot regions like the Southern Ocean, equatorial Pacific and Indian Ocean, northern Pacific near Alaska, and northern Atlantic near the British Islands. We analyzed the area-weighted annual time series of DMS concentrations in six different latitudinal bands (SI Appendix, Fig. S32), i.e., northern polar (90° N–60° N), northern mid-latitudes (60° N–30° N), northern equatorial (30° N–0° N), southern equatorial (0° S–30° S), southern mid-latitudes (30° S–60° S), and southern polar (60° S–90° S). On average, the northern polar region shows an increase in DMS in all the scenarios (6.21 to 8.30%, from SSP126 to SSP585) (SI Appendix, Fig. S32A), but a decrease is seen in other regions, for example, the southern polar region shows a large decreasing trend (−7.39% to −17.83%, from SSP126 to SSP585). Globally averaged, seawater DMS concentration decreases in future scenarios (Fig. 2A and SI Appendix, Fig. S21A).

caption.p: Geographical distribution in DMS concentrations and sea-air DMS flux decadal trends show that certain regions dominate the global decreasing/increasing trends. The geographical trends in (A) the multimodel ensemble seawater DMS concentrations and (B) DMS fluxes for the historical (1850 to 2014) and future (2015 to 2100) periods shows heterogenous spatial variability, with the overall decrease in average concentrations being dominated by some regions. The four future scenarios (SSP126, SSP245, SSP370, SSP585) are shown for the future periods. The regions marked with “x” indicate the areas where the trends are not significant (90%).

sec.title: Global and Regional Trends of Sea-Air DMS Fluxes.
sec.p: The total annual global DMS flux predicted by the ensemble of GPR models for the observation period (1972 to 2014) is 18.89 ± 0.28 Tg S y−1. Like seawater DMS concentrations, the sea-air fluxes (Fig. 2B) also show an increase in the recent past (1950 to 2014). Ice core studies have shown evidence of increasing biogenic sulfur fluxes in the industrial period around the Arctic (45) and the average biogenic sulfate was 16% higher over the Atlantic sector in the penultimate glacial period compared to the later last interglacial period (41). It has also been suggested that atmospheric methane sulfonic acid (MSA), an oxidation product of DMS, will increase in the future (46). Our results show that in addition to atmospheric processes, MSA concentrations will change due to increases in DMS sea-air fluxes driven by oceanic processes. Globally averaged, the trends of seawater DMS concentrations and the DMS sea-air fluxes are opposite to each other, with DMS fluxes showing an increase in all future scenarios (Fig. 2B). There is some variability observed between the models (SI Appendix, Fig. S21B), and the multimodel ensemble means shows a relative increase ranging from +1.6% (SSP126) to +3.7% (SSP585) (Fig. 2B) in 2085 to 2100 compared to 1850 to 1865.

In the recent historical past (Summary given in SI Appendix, Table S6), DMS fluxes show a much larger increase during 1950 to 2014 (+0.26 ± 0.02% decade−1) than during 1850 to 1900 (+0.04 ± 0.03% decade−1) and 1900 to 1950 (+0.08 ± 0.02% decade−1). In the SSP126 scenario (SI Appendix, Fig. S23), in the near future (2015 to 2050), neither the DMS flux trend nor the wind speed trends are significant, but the seawater DMS concentrations show a significant decreasing trend (−0.41 ± 0.04% decade−1). This is similar for the SSP245 (SI Appendix, Fig. S24) and SSP370 (SI Appendix, Fig. S25) scenarios. However, at the end of SSP585 (SI Appendix, Fig. S26), the DMS flux is significantly increasing (+0.37 ± 0.03% decade−1) in response to increasing SST (+3.41 ± 0.04% decade−1) and surface windspeed (+0.06 ± 0.02% decade−1), despite decreasing seawater DMS (−0.58 ± 0.02% decade−1). The increase in DMS fluxes in the historical period is driven by the increase in seawater DMS concentrations along with windspeed and SST (SI Appendix, Fig. S33). However, in the future, the change in windspeed (SI Appendix, Fig. S34) and SST drive the increase in DMS fluxes. Interestingly, the largest increases in DMS fluxes are projected to occur in the southern mid-latitudes and the northern polar regions (Fig. 3B), where DMS can have the largest climatic influence due to low anthropogenic sources of aerosols (47).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Currently, four CMIP6 models predict DMS using prognostic and diagnostic approaches(8). These models show large differences in the average predicted DMS seawater concentrations, the sea-air fluxes, and their temporal evolution. For the period of 2085 to 2100, CNRM-ESM2-1 (prognostic) and MIROC-ES2L (diagnostic) project an increase of 7.92% and 10.23% (SSP585), while NorESM2-MM (prognostic) and UKESM1-0-LL (diagnostic) project a decrease of −7.31% and −11.79% when compared to 1850 to 1865 (SI Appendix, Fig. S35A). The multimodel ensemble mean results in the trends canceling each other (SI Appendix, Fig. S35B). Conversely, the GPR models consistently project a decreasing trend in seawater DMS for all models, including the four models mentioned above (Fig. 2A and SI Appendix, Fig. S21A). This suggests that the differences in the current CMIP6 biogeochemical model DMS predictions arise from differences in the representation of the DMS production, loss, or concentration parameterizations (SI Appendix, Table S3). It is noteworthy that even though the predictor variables used by GPR models differ for each CMIP6 model, the ensemble projected DMS trend shows much less scatter than that from the current parameterizations in CMIP6 models. This is because diagnostic methods are currently too simplified, and prognostic methods still lack the detailed understanding and required complexity in the parameterizations to adequately simulate DMS on a global scale, while GPR models follow an approach in which the model is trained with predictor variables without making any assumptions about the underlying form of predictors i.e., linearity with DMS observations, which results in less generalization and lower errors. Hence, the GPR models can better handle the nonlinear relationship between predictor variables and DMS, resulting in a more trustworthy simulation closer to the observations, while the prognostic models are bound to the prescribed assumptions for capturing nonlinear relationships. The presented approach decreases the uncertainty in the seawater DMS projections: GPR implementation reduces the spread between models by 80% for the historical period, 69% for SSP126 and 68% for SSP585 future scenarios (2015 to 2100) (SI Appendix, Fig. S36). Noteworthily, in the present period, GPR models perform better than previous estimates (26, 34) in the Southern Ocean, a region with lower sources of anthropogenically derived aerosols, hence where biogenic marine sulfur fluxes have the largest climatic influence (5, 48, 49).

This work reduces the previously shown large differences in the projected seawater DMS and flux estimates among the CMIP6 models. Implementation of GPR-based prediction of DMS using the predictors from CMIP6 models reduces the scatter between models and improves the simulation of observations worldwide. Globally averaged surface-ocean DMS concentrations are projected to decrease in the most global warming scenarios, particularly because of decreases in the equatorial, subboreal, and high-latitude Southern Ocean waters, while increases are projected in the Arctic and part of subtropical waters. Despite this overall decrease in DMS concentration, DMS emissions are projected to increase, amplified by increases in windspeed and SST. This contradicts the current assessment in the IPCC AR6, which suggests a decrease in DMS fluxes in the future(23). Larger DMS emissions imply that biogenic sulfur from the ocean will play an increasingly more important role in the Earth’s radiation budget as air quality measures reduce anthropogenic sulfur emissions(50). Our study provides the means for better-constrained DMS emissions to be included in climate modeling studies.

sec.title: Data Description.
sec.p: Raw in situ seawater DMS concentration data is collected from various ocean cruises across the globe (51–54). A total of 855,652 in situ DMS data points are used from different ocean cruises. The predictors, as selected by Wang et al. (26) were initially considered for this study and were downloaded from different CMIP6 models (55). The list of predictors and the corresponding CMIP6 models is shown in SI Appendix, Table S1.

After removing missing values from the observed DMS dataset and considering a lower threshold of 0.1 nM, 853,343 DMS data points (Fig. 1A) are used in further processing. Predictor variables used in this study (SI Appendix, Table S1) are at resolutions (spatial and temporal) according to CMIP6 models. Hence, the in situ DMS data (Fig. 1A) are binned to the respective model resolution and averaged monthly. The final DMS data points for each model are shown in SI Appendix, Table S4. DMS data points from 1972 to 2014 are used to train the machine learning model along with predictors in SI Appendix, Table S1 as a “historical scenario” for each model, which is only up to 2014, and DMS in situ observations are present from 1972 onward. The machine learning model utilized in this study is Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), which has demonstrated superior performance in predicting seawater DMS (30) and atmospheric sulfur aerosol concentrations (56). Notably, GPR is effective in handling limited datasets and capturing complex spatiotemporal patterns in observational datasets (57). The DMS observations and predictors from the CMIP6 models were separated according to different ocean biomes for training the GPR models.

The predictors from CMIP6 models also have a few negative values for some months, which is not possible; hence, those values are replaced by the smallest positive value from the closest grid box. The UKESM1-0-LL model does not have net downward shortwave radiation at the surface (rsntds, W m−2). In this case, the downwelling shortwave flux in seawater (rsdo, W m−2) was used. In this study both rsntds and rsdo are denoted as Rad (See the second column in SI Appendix, Table S1). The sea surface wind speed (sfcWind: denoted as windspeed) data are used to calculate the sea-air flux along with other variables needed in the parameterization as described below. Only the windspeed data from MIROC-ES2L is not used to calculate the ensemble windspeed as the data is not available for all future scenarios. Similarly, MLD and SiO4 are also not available for MIROC-ES2L for all the scenarios (SI Appendix, Table S2).

sec.title: Backward Stepwise Regression and Predictors Selection.
sec.p: Before training the GPR models for each biome we applied the backward stepwise regression and ANOVA for each dataset to select the significant predictors of DMS for each biome [as mentioned by Mansour et al. (30)]. In backward stepwise regression, an individual predictor is removed from each model dataset to check its contribution to the total R2. If the R2 is drastically reduced due to the removal of a predictor, it is considered to be an important predictor. From the ANOVA, the predictors which show P < 0.05 are kept for training the GPR model of the different biomes. The results from the ANOVA and the selected variables for each case are given in SI Appendix, Table S2.

sec.title: Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) Models.
sec.p: The training of a single global machine learning model on a global scale for predicting seawater DMS concentrations can underestimate the small variations that occur in different biomes. Accordingly, our study separated the in situ DMS observations and predictors into four different biomes (Coastal, Polar, Trade, and Westerlies). The data in each biome are further separated into training (80% of the data points) and test subsets (20% of the data points), as shown in SI Appendix, Table S4. This study employs a GPR model for each biome that can independently train and predict seawater DMS concentrations. While training, a k-fold cross-validation is applied to the training dataset. This means that the training dataset is divided into k groups. In each trial, k-1 groups are used to train the GPR model, and the independent remaining group is used to validate the model. Finally, the average evaluation of the trials is reported as the training RMSE. The models are trained and optimized to achieve the lowest RMSE. Apart from cross-validation, a test dataset, which is unknown for the GPR model, is used to further evaluate the performance of the GPR model and ensure the repeatability on new data. After this, each biome's final model is saved to generate gridded fields of DMS covering the whole biome derived by the CMIP6 predictors. Results from the four GPR predicted outputs are then combined to get the global seawater DMS concentrations of past, present, and future scenarios. The final predicted seawater DMS concentration is at the same spatial and temporal resolution as the predictors from each of the CMIP6 models. We predict the seawater DMS concentrations for four shared socioeconomic pathways:

list-item.p: SSP1-2.6: CO2 emissions will reach net zero after 2050; temperatures stabilize at 1.8 °C by 2100.
SSP2-4.5: CO2 emissions hovering near current levels before declining midcentury; temperatures rise by 2.7 °C by 2100.
SSP3-7.0: CO2 emissions will double from current levels by 2100; temperatures will rise by 3.6 °C by 2100.
SSP5-8.5: CO2 emissions will double by 2050; temperature will soar by 4.4 °C by 2100.

sec.p: For final analysis and comparisons, the predicted seawater DMS outputs are regridded to a 1° × 1° spatial resolution using distance-weighted interpolation. After this, the ensemble means of all eight CMIP6 models (58) is calculated along with the standard deviation (SD).

sec.title: SHAP Analysis.
sec.p: Machine learning models are powerful tools to predict seawater DMS but can be hard to interpret. However, the SHAP values can help measure how much each predictor contributes to the model’s prediction. The SHAP values are based on game theory, and they assign an importance value (positive or negative) to each predictor of the model (59). The mean of the absolute SHAP values assigned to each predictor measures how strong the effect of the predictor is on the final output. The SHAP analysis can provide both global and local importance of the predictor (46). So, at every grid point, the value of seawater DMS concentration is due to the overall contribution of the predictors with some of the predictors contributing more compared to others. We calculated the SHAP values for the training dataset of every model in each biome. The mean of the absolute SHAP value of the predictor calculates how strong the predictor is compared to other predictors, but it does not show its positive or negative contribution. Hence, we also plotted the swarm charts, which helps to understand the positive or negative impact of individual predictors based on the SHAP value assigned to that predictor. This analysis is done for each biome and with each model predictor.

sec.title: Flux Parameterization.
sec.p: The sea-air DMS fluxes are calculated using the predicted seawater DMS of our model and an ensemble of windspeed data and SST. The input variables for the flux parameterization equation are regridded to a 2.5° × 2.5° resolution as windspeed data from the CMIP6 models mentioned in SI Appendix, Table S1 are at a coarser resolution compared to other variables (SST and predicted seawater DMS). After regridding the DMS data from 1° × 1° to 2.5° × 2.5°, the distribution does not show any large differences (SI Appendix, Fig. S37). The final sea-air flux is calculated using F = k (Cw - Ca/H). Generally, the waterside DMS concentrations (Cw) are several orders of magnitude higher than DMS in the atmosphere (Ca). Hence, Ca/H is ignored (60). The total gas transfer velocity (k) in cm h−1 is given as[1]k=1kw+Hka-1.
Here, ka is the airside transfer velocity in cm h−1 which is calculated following Yang et al. (61),[2]ka=8814×U∗+6810×(U∗)2.
Here, U* is calculated as per Johnson et al. (62),[3]U∗=Windspeed×1.3×10-3.
The waterside transfer velocity (kw) in cm h−1 is calculated following Marandino et al. (13), which is given as,[4]kw=0.46×Windspeed-0.24×10024×Sc720-0.5.
Here, Sc is the Schmidt number which depends upon the SST which is calculated as per Saltzman et al. (63),[5]Sc=2674-147.12×SST+3.726×SST2-0.038×SST3.

In Eq. 4, kw depends linearly on the 10 m surface windspeed, so no correction for wind speed distribution is needed as happens with quadratic schemes. Expressing kw as a function of windspeed rather than friction velocity avoids uncertainties related to the drag coefficients (5).

To ensure no negative DMS flux values, kw <= 0 is set to 1.1 cm h−1. The Henry’s constant (H) in Eq. 1 is the dimensionless water over air solubility of DMS and is a function of SST as follows,[6]H=0.0053×e3500×1SST+273.15-1291.15×0.08206×SST+273.15×101.325.
Finally, sea-air DMS flux (F) is calculated in µmol m−2 d−1 using the Eq. 7,[7]F=k×Cw×0.24.

sec.title: Trend Analysis.
sec.p: Considering the changing studied predictor variables across the 250 y period, we break the time series of predicted seawater DMS, DMS flux, Windspeed, and SST into sections of 50 y for both the historical and future scenarios (except 1950 to 2014 which is > 50 y). The trend in each section is calculated using the bootstrap resampling method (7, 14, 64). The validity of the trend is determined by the significance value which is denoted as tB in the bootstrap method. If tB is more than 2, then the significance of the slope is better than 95%. The value of tB is calculated as the ratio of the mean trend and mean SD (65).

We also calculated the spatial trends of predicted seawater DMS concentrations and DMS fluxes for historical and future scenarios. As given by Bock et al. (8), the spatial trends are calculated using a linear statistical model from which the slope (trend) and P-value (statistical significance) are obtained for every grid point. We considered as significant only those grid points with P-value < 0.1 and marked the grid points with x which shows significance less than 90%.

ack.p: The Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology is funded by the Ministry of Earth Sciences, Government of India. We thank the Scientific Committee on Oceanic Research (SCOR) and the U.S. NSF through a grant to SCOR (Grant OCE-2140395) for financial support. R.S. holds a European Research Council Advanced Grant (SUMMIT, ERC-2018-AdG-834162). R.S. and M.G are funded by the Spanish Ministry of Science and Innovation (MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033) through grant PID2022-140872NB-I00 (GOOSE) and “Severo Ochoa Centre of Excellence” CEX2019-000928-S (ICM). N.S. acknowledges funding from Fisheries and Oceans Canada. We acknowledge the Surface Ocean-Lower Atmosphere Study and SCOR-working groups Coupling of Ocean Ice Atmosphere Processes from Sea-ice Biogeochemistry to Aerosols and Clouds (CIce2Clouds) and Developing Resources for the Study of Methylated Sulfur Compound Cycling Processes in the Ocean (DMS-PRO). We thank all the groups that have contributed their measurements to the Global Surface Seawater Dimethyl sulfide Database.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: A.S.M. designed research; S.D.J., K.M., R.S., M.G., N.S., A.S.-L., and A.S.M. performed research; S.D.J., K.M., and A.S.M. contributed new reagents/analytic tools; S.D.J., R.S., M.G., N.S., and A.S.M. analyzed data; and S.D.J., K.M., R.S., M.G., N.S., A.S.-L., and A.S.M. wrote the paper.

sec.title: Competing interests
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interest.

fn.p: This article is a PNAS Direct Submission.
Although PNAS asks authors to adhere to United Nations naming conventions for maps (https://www.un.org/geospatial/mapsgeo), our policy is to publish maps as provided by the authors.

sec.title: Data, Materials, and Software Availability
sec.p: The in situ DMS observations are available at PMEL (https://saga.pmel.noaa.gov/dms_data) (51), PANGAEA (https://www.pangaea.de/advanced/datawarehouse.php?q=Dimethyl+sulfide) (52) and NAAMES (https://seabass.gsfc.nasa.gov/experiment/NAAMES) (53) and the combined dataset is available at https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/hyn62spny2/2 (54). The predictor variables are downloaded from the CMIP6 website (https://aims2.llnl.gov/search) (55). The code used to produce the findings of this study along with the results are available at Mahajan (2025), “Data for Climate warming increases global oceanic dimethyl sulfide emissions”, Mendeley Data, V1, doi: https://doi.org/10.17632/prtjx8jjck.1 (58). All other data are included in the manuscript and/or SI Appendix.
